focus: Falsifiable dominant-child rule applied to all five auto-defaulting hubs with provisional keep-versus-null verdicts and explicit mis-default costs
noveltyJustification: All 6 findings are fully new: one cost-weighted decision rule and five evidence-backed per-hub verdicts.
keyQuestions: Five auto-defaulting hub verdicts and misroute costs
answeredQuestions: What evidence-backed decision rule distinguishes a genuine dominant child from a presumptive default?
Which of the five auto-defaulting hubs should keep its child default, and which should flip to null, based on mode mix and misroute cost?
ruledOut: Tie-break or registry order as evidence of dominance
Existing defaultMode as proof of genuine dominance
Mode count alone as the default policy

label: Keep a child default only when its cost-weighted zero-signal mis-default loss is lower than defer/detect loss; otherwise null is the burden-of-proof policy.
sk-prompt provisionally keeps prompt-improve because the parent declares it higher-traffic/general and recognizable model-profile requests score away from fallback.
cli-external-orchestration flips to null because three peer providers are equal-weight and wrong-provider/self-invocation costs exceed clarification.
system-deep-loop flips to null because seven equal-weight modes have incompatible state, convergence, artifact, and permission contracts.
mcp-tooling flips to null because six equal-weight tools span distinct systems and its fallback resource is already authored as a defer-time suggestion only.
sk-design flips to null because required manager intake and materially distinct modes make an interface guess costlier than one focused question.
Dominance must be evaluated on the no-discriminating-signal population after explicit hints, commands, and detection, not on total hub traffic.
Fleet verdict set is one keep (sk-prompt) and four nulls (CLI, deep-loop, MCP, design).

direction: Use tie-break or registry order as dominance evidence
reason: Equal-weight peer modes show ordering is deterministic resolution, not traffic or expected-loss evidence.

direction: Treat current defaultMode as proof of dominance
reason: Several parent contracts explicitly defer on ambiguity despite the named child default.

direction: Choose policy from mode count alone
reason: The two null controls show unresolved choice and detection behavior, not count, determine safe fallback.
